title: Scaleway secret keys should not be disclosed
description: Scaleway API credentials (Access Keys and Secret Keys) provide programmatic access to Scaleway cloud infrastructure including compute instances (DEV1, GP1, RENDER), object storage, databases, Kubernetes clusters, load balancers, and networking resources. When these credentials are hardcoded in source code, configuration files, or version control systems, they create critical security vulnerabilities. Exposed credentials can allow unauthorized users to create, modify, or delete cloud infrastructure, access sensitive data stored in Scaleway Object Storage, manipulate databases and Kubernetes clusters, and incur unexpected charges. Scaleway Secret Keys are typically in UUID format (36 characters with hyphens) or long alphanumeric strings (40-64 characters). Trust boundaries are violated when these secrets are exposed in uncontrolled environments.
recommendation: Remove hardcoded Scaleway secret keys from source code immediately. Use environment variables (SCW_SECRET_KEY), secure secret management services like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. Revoke any exposed keys through the Scaleway Console (IAM → API Keys). Regenerate new secret keys and update all legitimate services. Consider using scoped API keys with minimal required permissions (read-only, specific project access) and implement proper secret rotation policies. Enable access logs and monitoring for unauthorized API usage.
impact: Critical security risk - unauthorized access to Scaleway cloud infrastructure, potential resource manipulation, data breaches, infrastructure deletion, and unexpected billing charges

compliant.description: Reading secret key from environment variable

checks.message: Scaleway secret key detected in source code (UUID format). Scaleway secret keys provide authentication to cloud infrastructure management APIs for compute instances, object storage, databases, Kubernetes clusters, and networking resources. Exposing these keys allows unauthorized users to: create/delete/modify cloud infrastructure consuming your credits, access Object Storage buckets and their contents, manipulate managed databases and backups, control Kubernetes clusters and container workloads, modify DNS zones and domain configurations, access load balancers and private networks, view billing information and account details, and launch compute instances for cryptomining or DDoS attacks. Store secret keys securely using environment variables (SCW_SECRET_KEY, SCW_ACCESS_KEY) or secret management systems (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault). IMMEDIATELY revoke exposed keys from Scaleway Console (console.scaleway.com → IAM → API Keys) and regenerate new credentials. Enable access logging, use scoped API keys with minimal permissions, restrict keys to specific projects, implement IP whitelisting, and rotate keys regularly.

Scaleway secret key detected in source code (alphanumeric format). This secret key grants full access to your Scaleway infrastructure management APIs. Attackers can exploit exposed secret keys to: provision expensive compute resources (GP1-XL, RENDER-S instances) for cryptocurrency mining operations consuming your credits rapidly, access and exfiltrate data from Object Storage buckets containing backups or sensitive files, delete or modify production databases and their snapshots causing data loss, manipulate Kubernetes clusters injecting malicious containers or stealing secrets, modify DNS zones redirecting traffic to malicious servers, access Container Registry images and inject backdoored containers, modify load balancer configurations disrupting service availability, access private networks and VPC configurations exposing internal infrastructure, and view account billing information revealing usage patterns. IMMEDIATELY revoke the exposed secret key from console.scaleway.com (IAM → API Keys → Delete), regenerate new credentials, update all services and CI/CD pipelines with new keys, review access logs for unauthorized activity (API access logs, compute instance creation, Object Storage access), check for unexpected resource creation or billing charges, rotate keys in all affected environments (development, staging, production), and implement secret scanning in CI/CD pipelines to prevent future exposures.
